metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the filesystem-copy approach with `git clone --local --branch tickets <REAL_REPO_ROOT> <TMP_DIR>` (or `git clone --mirror` + checkout) to create a proper independent git clone with its own .git directory, so rebar edit commits on the tmp store are isolated from the real tickets branch.
evidence: Plan says: 'the harness copies REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT/.tickets-tracker → TMP_MUTANT_STORE (a full clone)'
init.py:218: `if os.path.isdir(tracker) and os.path.isfile(os.path.join(tracker, '.git'))` — the .git entry is a FILE (linked worktree), not a directory
init.py:347: `cp = _git(repo, 'worktree', 'add', tracker, branch)` — confirmed: tracker is created via git worktree add

gate_source.py:104-117: local mode does NOT call use_tickets_root, so ticket reads go through config.tracker_dir() → REBAR_TRACKER_DIR (correct), but git commits on the copied dir still write to the real repo's git dir via the .git file pointer
push.py:1-5: REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off disables git push, NOT git commit — local commits to the real tickets branch are not blocked
findings.finding: The 'copy .tickets-tracker → TMP_MUTANT_STORE' step in the gate-execution mechanism does not produce an isolated git store: .tickets-tracker is a linked worktree (git worktree add, confirmed at init.py:218,347-367) whose .git entry is a FILE pointing to the real repo's .git/worktrees/tickets. A filesystem copy leaves this pointer intact, so git commands run against the copy (-C TMP_MUTANT_STORE) resolve to the REAL repo's git object store. The rebar edit injection step (git commit) would then commit the seeded mutation to the real tickets branch — violating the 'real store untouched' invariant. REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off blocks git push (remote) only, not git commit (local), so HEAD of the real store would advance. The isolation AC's HEAD check would detect this after the fact, but the contamination has already occurred.
findings.location: ## What — 'Mutant store = a clone of the real store (real store untouched)' paragraph; run_e2_benchmark.py scope item
scenarios: Harness runs rebar edit <id> --description=<mutated_plan> with REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=TMP_MUTANT_STORE; git -C TMP_MUTANT_STORE reads .git file → follows gitdir: pointer to REAL_REPO/.git/worktrees/tickets; git commit writes a new commit to the real tickets branch; the seeded mutation becomes a real event in the live store.
The isolation AC (H0=$(git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD); run; H1=...; [ $H0 = $H1 ]) detects H0 ≠ H1 and fails — but the tickets branch is already contaminated with mutant EDIT events.
findings.suggested_fix:
In run_e2_benchmark.py, replace `shutil.copytree(real_tracker, tmp_store)` (or equivalent cp -r) with:
```python
import subprocess, tempfile
tmp_store = tempfile.mkdtemp()
subprocess.run(['git', 'clone', '--local', '--branch', 'tickets', real_repo_root, tmp_store], check=True)
```
This creates a standalone clone with its own .git directory, so all git operations (-C tmp_store) are fully isolated. The tickets branch content is identical to the source; REBAR_TRACKER_DIR can then point at tmp_store safely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or a run-validity sub-check that verifies the gate received the mutated plan: for at least one 'smoke-test' pair where the mutation is deliberately constructed to guarantee a BLOCK verdict (e.g., a class-2 mutant known to trigger an existing `G3`/`G4` criterion), assert the mutant verdict differs from its control verdict, confirming `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` routing is active.
evidence: AC 4 checks e2_baseline.json fields and that the run was valid (indeterminate fractions), but does NOT check that any mutant produced a different verdict than its paired control (which would confirm the gate saw the mutation).
AC 7 checks 'the real .tickets-tracker branch HEAD unchanged' — this only proves no writes to the real store, not that reads came from the clone.
The plan states 'REBAR_TRACKER_DIR redirects assemble_context's show_ticket/list_tickets to the tmp mutant store' but no AC proves this redirection is in effect during the gate run.
findings.finding: No AC verifies that the gate actually received and processed the MUTATED plan text (i.e., that `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` routing to the clone is functioning). The isolation AC (AC 7) checks only that the real store's HEAD is unchanged after a run — it does not confirm that the mutant's modified description was the input to `review_plan`. A broken routing (e.g., `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` silently ignored, gate falling back to real store) would produce systematically low detection rates indistinguishable from 'the gate legitimately cannot catch this class,' silently producing an invalid baseline without triggering any AC failure.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 and AC 7
scenarios: REBAR_TRACKER_DIR is set but a code path in assemble_context reads from a hardcoded default; the gate scores the original unmodified plan for every 'mutant' row; the baseline shows near-zero detection for all classes; this is attributed to 'expected low baseline for miss classes' and committed as valid.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s `run_validity` logic (or as a pre-flight check in `--self-check-isolation`): select the class-2 or class-3 smoke-test pair whose baseline detection is expected 'partial' (not near-zero), run it, and assert `mutant_verdict != control_verdict`. Record this check in `e2_run_validity.json` as `{routing_smoke_test: {mutant_verdict, control_verdict, routing_confirmed: bool}}`. Fail the run non-zero if `routing_confirmed` is False.

findings.finding: The mutation-assembly logic in `assemble_e2_mutants.py` for defect classes 1–4 has no per-class construct-validity test: AC 1's prove command only checks row counts, pairing, required fields, and the class-5 tag strip — it does NOT assert that class-1 mutations actually introduce an absent-capability assertion, that class-2 produces layer-partitioned (not vertically-sliced) children, that class-3 names an artifact without a `depends_on`/`blocks` edge, or that class-4 introduces an unnecessary mechanism. If the mutation logic is subtly wrong for any of these classes, the baseline silently measures the wrong defect, invalidating downstream R1/R3/R4 benchmarks.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 prove command; `assemble_e2_mutants.py` scope entry; defect classes 1–4 mutation definitions in ## What
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean` (AC 2) or add a dedicated `--verify-mutations` flag that, for each committed mutant row, runs a per-class semantic check function and exits non-zero naming the first failing pair. Mirror AC 1's class-5 prove pattern: for class 3, assert `any(edge in control_children for edge in ['depends_on','blocks']) and not any(edge in mutant_children ...)` etc. Add the four class checks to `test_e2_counting.py` or a new `test_e2_mutations.py` using synthetic corpus rows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` (alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`) in the `run_e2_benchmark.py` harness env setup, document it in the gate-execution mechanism section, and add it to the isolation AC's proving command — otherwise every `show_ticket`/`list_tickets` call triggers a live `git fetch` from the tmp clone against the real remote.
evidence: Plan sets `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` but never mentions `REBAR_SYNC_PULL`
`src/rebar/_reads.py:37-39`: `def _fresh(tracker): ticket_reads.ensure_fresh(tracker)` called on every `show_ticket` / `list_tickets`
`src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:171-183`: `_sync_disabled` calls `load_config(root=os.path.dirname(tracker_abs))`; ConfigError → returns False (sync enabled)
`src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:209-215`: `git -C <tracker_abs> rev-parse --verify <branch>` subprocess executed; if branch exists in the clone, the fetch proceeds
Plan's isolation AC only proves `git -C .tickets-tracker rev-parse HEAD` is unchanged (write isolation) — it does not prevent read-path fetches from the tmp clone's origin
findings.finding: The 'verified gate-run mechanism' sets `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` (write isolation) but omits `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off`. The production `_reads.show_ticket` and `list_tickets` calls trigger `ensure_fresh()` → a throttled `git fetch origin tickets` inside the tmp clone. When `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` is a tmp directory, `_sync_disabled` resolves `load_config(root=os.path.dirname(tmp_path))` which fails (no rebar.toml in tmp parent) and returns `sync enabled`, so every first read within a 60-second window per unique tmp path issues a live `git fetch` against the real remote — a live-network side-channel that violates the store-isolation property the plan asserts, causes failures in offline/CI environments, and adds latency across 20+ mutant/control pairs.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section; Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py description; AC: 'ISOLATION-OK' check
scenarios: CI / offline environments: `git fetch origin tickets` inside the tmp clone fails with a network error; `ensure_fresh` swallows it (best-effort), so reads still succeed, but each run logs spurious network errors and any per-run timing assertions are polluted.

Performance: 20+ mutant/control pairs with distinct tmp dirs each get their own throttle marker path, so the 60-second throttle provides no cross-pair benefit — every pair's first read triggers a fetch.
Remote rate-limiting: if the remote throttles fetch bursts (e.g., GitHub's API rate limits for LFS/smart-HTTP), the harness silently degrades baseline quality without surfacing the cause.
findings.suggested_fix: In `run_e2_benchmark.py`, set both `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` and `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` before injecting `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`. Update the README's gate-run-mechanism description and the isolation-AC proving command to assert both are set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (alongside `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`) to the harness's non-mutating env flags — both in the ## What pseudocode block and in the `run_e2_benchmark.py` scope description — to prevent ensure_fresh() from fetching live remote changes into the tmp mutant store mid-run.
evidence: Plan states: 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off' but does not mention REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off
src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-251: ensure_fresh() runs git fetch + reconverge on every show_ticket call unless REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off or _sync_disabled()
src/rebar/_reads.py:14: 'Opt out with REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off'
ABSENCE: REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off is not mentioned anywhere in the plan's mechanism description or the harness pseudocode
findings.finding: The harness sets REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off but does not set REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off. In local mode, every review_plan() call triggers _reads.show_ticket() → ensure_fresh() (src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py:186-251), which attempts a throttled 'git fetch origin tickets' + reconverge against the tmp mutant store. The tmp clone is a directory copy of .tickets-tracker and retains the real upstream remote; with pull sync enabled, each gate run may fetch live remote changes into the mutant store mid-benchmark, silently overwriting the injected mutations and undermining the store-isolation guarantee the plan's own AC checks for.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism / 'Non-mutating flags' bullet; AC: store-isolation prove command
scenarios: A benchmark run over ≥100 mutant/control pairs triggers ≥100 ensure_fresh() calls; if the throttle marker is new-keyed per the tmp clone's md5, each call may attempt a live fetch, potentially reverting injected description mutations before the gate reads them.
Even if fetch fails silently, the reconverge step runs against the cloned store structure and could reset locally-written ticket events to their pre-injection state.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PULL"] = "off"` immediately after `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` in the harness setup, and add it to the 'Non-mutating flags' bullet in ## What: `REBAR_SYNC_PULL=off` (suppresses git fetch + reconverge on every show_ticket read so pull-sync cannot overwrite injected mutations in the tmp clone).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC (or a test case in `test_e2_benchmark.py`) that proves crash-resumability — e.g. a fixture that pre-populates `e2_verdicts.jsonl` with some rows and asserts the harness skips those rows — or explicitly downgrade 'crash-resumable' to a non-required implementation note in Scope.
evidence: Scope: 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`, crash-resumable)'
ABSENCE: no AC includes a prove-command that kills the harness mid-run and re-runs it, asserting previously-completed rows are not re-executed or re-submitted to the gate
findings.finding: The crash-resumable behavior of `run_e2_benchmark.py` ('journals raw verdicts incrementally (`e2_verdicts.jsonl`, crash-resumable)') is a described deliverable in Scope with no covering AC. No prove-command or test asserts that a partial `e2_verdicts.jsonl` allows the harness to skip already-completed rows on re-run.
findings.location: Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC4, AC5, AC6, AC7 (none covers crash-resume)
scenarios: Implementer skips crash-resume logic since no AC enforces it; a long benchmark run that crashes must restart from scratch, potentially re-running expensive gate calls and producing duplicate rows in e2_verdicts.jsonl

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `passes.py:269,514` citation with the actual lines that route agentic file-tool access through `cfg.repo_path` in local mode (e.g., `RunnerAgentStep` in `gate_dispatch.py` / the agentic criterion dispatch) and verify that `source='local'` + `repo_root=REAL_REPO` actually causes the agentic grounding file tools to read the real checkout rather than some other path.
evidence: Plan text: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'
passes.py:269 actual content: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — prompt fetching inside `_resolve_system()`
passes.py:514 actual content: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` — ISF summarizer prompt fetching
Agentic code grounding routes through `RunnerAgentStep` in the workflow engine, not these lines
findings.finding: The plan claims '`passes.py:269,514`' is where 'agentic finders read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading `passes.py` at those exact lines: line 269 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (prompt resolution inside `_resolve_system`), and line 514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (ISF log summarizer). Neither line is an agentic finder reading real codebase modules. The actual agentic code-grounding path flows through the workflow engine's `RunnerAgentStep` (not exposed at these lines), so the cited evidence for the architectural claim is wrong.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules'
scenarios: An implementer trusting these line citations to verify that local+REAL_REPO code-grounding works would examine prompt-fetching code and find no agentic module-reading logic, undermining confidence in the mechanism and possibly causing them to mis-implement the harness.
A reviewer of R1/R3/R4 who traces back the E2 evidence chain to verify code-grounding semantics would find the cited lines do not support the claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `det_floor.py:345,392` citation: those lines are P2 (file-path resolution) and P3 (package existence) probes, neither of which contributes to class-1 or class-4 detection; cite instead the LLM/agentic path (E4/G1G2/G6 criteria dispatch) as the source of class-1/4 grounding.
evidence: Plan text: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) and the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules — which is what makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'
det_floor.py:345 actual content: P2 file-reference probe — `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'file', 'name': ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)`
det_floor.py:392 actual content: P3 package-existence probe — `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'dependency', 'name': pkg}, ...)`
The current-gate baseline table for class 1 correctly lists `E4`, `G1G2`, `G6` (agentic) as the nearest criteria — confirming class-1 detection is LLM/agentic, not these P2/P3 DET checks
findings.finding: The plan describes `det_floor.py:345,392` as 'the DET-floor absence probe' that 'makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real.' Reading the file: line 345 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'file', 'name': ref}, repo_root=ctx.repo_root)` (P2 file-path resolution — checks whether a file a plan REFERENCES exists) and line 392 is `grounding.refute_absence({'kind': 'dependency', 'name': pkg}, ...)` (P3 package existence). Neither performs absent-capability probing or unnecessary-mechanism detection. Class-1 (absent asserted capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) are LLM-driven via E4, G1G2, G6, and the agentic verifier — not these DET-floor file/package probes.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: 'the DET-floor absence probe (`det_floor.py:345,392`) … makes class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding real'
scenarios: An implementer may believe the DET floor independently probes absent capabilities (making class-1 baseline detection non-zero), but P2/P3 never block and only measure file/package presence — not the asserted-capability pattern.
The run-validity reasoning about why class-1 baseline is 'expected low' could be confused if DET-floor probes were mistakenly thought to catch it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the misattributed `passes.py:269,514` citation with the actual code paths through which `repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT` reaches the agentic finders — e.g., `review_plan → assemble_context → PlanContext.repo_root` (orchestrator.py:195-203) and `cfg.repo_path` read by AGENT-tier criteria (registry.py:66).
evidence: passes.py:269 actual content: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system` — prompt library lookup using repo_path to find custom prompt overrides in `.rebar/prompts/`
passes.py:514 actual content: `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `summarize_for_isf` — same pattern, unrelated to agentic code finding

The actual agentic code-grounding runs through PlanContext.repo_root (set from review_plan's repo_root arg) and cfg.repo_path, consumed by the AGENT-tier criteria (G1G2, E4, A1, G6 per registry.py:66)
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the agentic finders (`passes.py:269,514`) read the REAL modules' as the mechanism by which class-1 (absent capability) and class-4 (unnecessary mechanism) grounding is code-real. But line 269 of passes.py is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt(prompt_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `_resolve_system` (prompt library lookup), and line 514 is `prompt = prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-isf-summarizer', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` inside `summarize_for_isf` (also a prompt lookup). Neither line is an agentic code-grounding finder. The `repo_root` argument at those lines resolves prompt-override files from `.rebar/prompts/`, not the codebase for capability checking. An implementer auditing the mechanism against these citations would find a mismatch.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism section: '(b) code-grounding = the real repo: … the DET-floor absence probe (det_floor.py:345,392) and the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules'
scenarios: An implementer verifying the harness mechanism against the cited lines finds prompt-resolution code, not code-grounding logic, and incorrectly concludes the mechanism is broken or that a different harness wiring is needed.
A reviewer auditing whether class-1/4 mutant detection is truly code-grounded cannot trace the claim through the cited evidence.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`the agentic finders (passes.py:269,514) read the REAL modules`' with '`review_plan(…, repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)` sets PlanContext.repo_root (orchestrator.py:195-203) and cfg.repo_path, which the AGENT-tier criteria (G1G2, E4, A1, G6 — registry.py:66) use for code grounding; det_floor.py:345,392 use ctx.repo_root for the DET-floor absence probes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement the `e2_verdicts.jsonl` re-run idempotency policy: either (a) overwrite on every fresh run and skip resume, or (b) append-with-deduplication by `pair_id` (crash-resume skips already-logged `pair_id`s at startup), and add a duplicate-`pair_id` assertion to the AC 4 prove command.
evidence: Plan says 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' — but 'crash-resumable' implies restart capability without specifying append-vs-overwrite or deduplication
AC 4 prove command counts `len(vs)` rows but does not check for duplicate `pair_id` entries, so duplicates would pass the AC
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how the `e2_verdicts.jsonl` incremental journal handles re-runs: if the harness is restarted after a partial run, it is unspecified whether it appends to or overwrites the existing file. Appending without deduplication would produce duplicate `pair_id` rows, causing the baseline computation to double-count certain mutants/controls and produce incorrect detection rates.
findings.location: ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 4 (BASELINE-OK prove command)
scenarios: Harness crashes at row 47, leaving 46 rows in `e2_verdicts.jsonl`; on restart it appends another 100 rows (including re-run of the first 46), producing 146 rows where 46 pair_ids appear twice; `e2_baseline.json` double-counts those mutants.
findings.suggested_fix: At harness startup, read existing `e2_verdicts.jsonl` into a set of completed `pair_id`s; skip those pairs in the run loop; append only new rows. Add `assert len(vs) == len({json.loads(l)['pair_id'] for l in open(...)})` to the AC 4 prove command.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` flag description to the `run_e2_benchmark.py` Scope entry, specifying that it runs the full clone-inject-gate cycle on a single synthetic or first-corpus pair and exits 0, enabling the surrounding shell to verify the real store HEAD is unchanged.
evidence: Scope describes `run_e2_benchmark.py` with no mention of `--self-check-isolation`
AC7 prove-command: `.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` used to trigger the isolation check
ABSENCE: flag semantics (which pair to run, how to exit, what 'self-check' means) are unspecified in the plan body
findings.finding: The `--self-check-isolation` flag used in AC7's prove-command (`run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`) is not described in the Scope or What sections. Its required behavior (run a single-pair gate execution in the tmp clone and exit cleanly, allowing the surrounding shell to compare HEAD before/after) is implied only by the AC shell script. This is a testable seam with no specification in the plan body.
findings.location: Scope: `run_e2_benchmark.py` description; AC7 prove-command
scenarios: Implementer builds the harness per Scope but omits the `--self-check-isolation` mode; AC7 prove-command fails with unrecognised argument; no specification of what a 'self-check' run should do (one pair? first pair? synthetic pair?)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `run_e2_benchmark.py`, add: '`--self-check-isolation`: runs the tmp-store clone, injection, and gate call for exactly one pair (e.g. the first mutant/control pair in the corpus), then exits 0 without writing `e2_baseline.json` — intended to let the caller verify the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged before/after.'

findings.finding: AC7's prove command calls `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`, but the Scope section's description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` never mentions a `--self-check-isolation` CLI flag. The flag's behavior (running a single-pair run to verify the real store HEAD is unchanged) is only implied by the AC prove command itself, leaving it as undescribed work.
findings.location: Scope (run_e2_benchmark.py description) vs AC7 prove command
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope entry for run_e2_benchmark.py, add: '`--self-check-isolation`: runs a single mutant/control pair through the full mechanism and asserts the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged; exits 0 on success, non-zero on violation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `target_criteria` field per class to `runs/e2_thresholds.json` (e.g. `{"1": {"detection_min": 0.8, "blocking_fp_max": 0.1, "target_criteria": ["E4", "G1G2", "G6"]}, …}`) so the harness reads criterion ids from the pre-registered config rather than embedding them in source.
evidence: Plan defines `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` with 'a target-family finding surfaced with decision ∈ {block, advisory}' as the detection rule — `target_criteria` is a parameter but its per-class values are only in the prose table (Class 1 → E4, G1G2, G6; Class 2 → G3, G4; etc.).
`e2_thresholds.json` schema is defined as `{classes: {<id>: {detection_min, blocking_fp_max}}, indeterminate_cap}` — no `target_criteria` field is included.
No plan section says where the harness reads per-class target_criteria at runtime; the harness must either hard-code them or read them from a config, but neither is specified.
findings.finding: The `classify_pair(verdict_dict, target_criteria)` decision rule depends on knowing, per defect class, which criterion ids constitute the `target_criteria` set — but this per-class mapping is only in a prose table in `## What` and is never committed to any schema file co-located with the function definition or `e2_thresholds.json`.
findings.location: ## What — 'AMBIGUOUS / INDETERMINATE verdict handling' / `classify_pair` definition; baseline table listing E4/G1G2/G6 per class
scenarios: Implementer hard-codes criterion ids in `run_e2_benchmark.py` that diverge from those in the prose table, producing silently wrong attribution.
R-story re-runs using new criterion ids (proposed by R1/R3/R4) cannot override the target_criteria without modifying harness source — the mapping is not data-driven.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `e2_thresholds.json` schema spec in `## What` to include `target_criteria: [str]` per class entry. Update the AC3 prove command to assert each class entry contains a non-empty `target_criteria` list.

evidence: description is 26067 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the class-2 and class-3 mutation algorithms concretely in `assemble_e2_mutants.py`'s Scope description: for class 2, state the layer-detection heuristic (e.g., keyword matching on child titles/tags) and redistribution rule; for class 3, state whether the mutation (a) scans for existing cross-sibling artifact references and deletes only the dependency edge, or (b) injects a new cross-reference AND deletes the edge, and why.
evidence: 'layer-cake split — children re-partitioned by architectural layer (schema/backend/frontend/tests) so no single child yields an end-to-end observable outcome' — no procedure for how the script detects layer membership in arbitrary real plans

'consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge — a child's text names an artifact/interface a sibling creates, with NO depends_on/blocks edge or explicit sequencing' — describes the end state, not whether text is injected or only the edge is deleted
The Scope entry for assemble_e2_mutants.py says 'applies the per-class mechanical mutation' but gives no further detail on the class 2/3 algorithms
findings.finding: The mutation procedure for class 2 (layer-cake repartition) and class 3 (consumed-artifact-without-ordering-edge) is not specified concretely enough for `assemble_e2_mutants.py` to be implemented without a design choice. For class 2, repartitioning arbitrary real child tickets by 'architectural layer' requires the script to decide how to detect layer membership and redistribute children — real plans may have children that don't map cleanly to schema/backend/frontend/tests. For class 3, the plan states the mutant 'names an artifact/interface a sibling creates, with NO depends_on/blocks edge,' but doesn't specify whether the mutation is (a) find plans where sibling text already cross-references, then only delete the edge, or (b) inject new cross-reference text into a sibling AND delete the edge — these are fundamentally different procedures with different construct-validity implications.
findings.location: ## What — defect class definitions for classes 2 and 3; Scope — assemble_e2_mutants.py description
scenarios: An executor implementing assemble_e2_mutants.py must choose between inject-text vs. delete-edge-only for class 3, and must invent a layer-detection heuristic for class 2 — two separate design decisions not resolved in the plan.
If inject-text is chosen for class 3, construct validity differs from delete-edge-only (the former introduces fabricated cross-references not present in the original plan).
findings.suggested_fix: For class 3: adopt option (a) — scan real child descriptions for references to artifacts named in a sibling's description, select a pair where a dependency edge exists, and delete only the edge. This is maximally mechanical and construct-valid. State this in the Scope. For class 2: define 'layer' membership by a small keyword list (e.g., title/tag contains 'schema'|'migration' → data; 'api'|'route'|'handler' → backend; 'ui'|'component'|'page' → frontend; 'test'|'spec' → tests) and state the rule in the Scope. Add: 'if a base plan's children cannot be partitioned into ≥2 distinct layers by the keyword rule, skip it and select the next candidate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--self-check-isolation` mode to `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s Scope description, specifying what it does: runs exactly one mutant/control pair through the gate, then asserts the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged, and exits 0 on success.
evidence: AC 7 proving command: '.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation'

Scope entry for run_e2_benchmark.py: describes cloning, injecting, running review_plan, applying classify_pair, writing verdicts/validity/baseline — no mention of a --self-check-isolation flag or mode
findings.finding: AC 7 (store-isolation check) requires `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` to exist, but the Scope description of `run_e2_benchmark.py` does not mention this flag or the isolation self-check mode. An executor implementing the harness from the Scope description alone would omit this flag, causing AC 7's proving command to fail or error.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 7 (isolation); Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py description
scenarios: An executor builds run_e2_benchmark.py per the Scope, which has no --self-check-isolation argument; AC 7's proving command fails with 'unrecognized arguments: --self-check-isolation'.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for run_e2_benchmark.py, add: 'Accepts `--self-check-isolation`: runs a single hardcoded mutant/control pair (the first class-1 entry in the corpus) through the full gate mechanism, captures the HEAD of `.tickets-tracker` before and after, asserts equality, and exits 0 — used by AC 7's isolation proving command.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed `mutation_op` enum vocabulary (one value per defect class, e.g. `strip_capability_assertion | layer_cake_reslice | delete_ordering_edge | add_unnecessary_mechanism | strip_operator_attested_tag`) in `## What` or `runs/e2_thresholds.json`, and add a value-membership assertion to the AC1 prove command.
evidence: AC1 prove command asserts `mutation_op` exists per mutant row: `'mutation_op' + 'mutation_diff'` listed in the required fields, but no section of the plan names the allowed values (e.g. `strip_capability_assertion`, `layer_cake_reslice`, `delete_ordering_edge`, etc.).

The five defect classes are described in prose ('plan asserts an existing named module provides…', 'children re-partitioned by architectural layer…') but these prose descriptions are never mapped to enumerated constant strings.
No `mutation_op` vocabulary table appears in ## What, ## Scope, or any AC.
findings.finding: `mutation_op` is a required field in every mutant corpus row (named in AC1's schema and proven by the AC1 command), but the plan never defines its closed value vocabulary — the implementer of `assemble_e2_mutants.py` cannot determine what string constants to write, and a consumer of the corpus cannot validate them.
findings.location: ## What — corpus row schema / AC1 (`mutation_op` + `mutation_diff` field list)
scenarios: Implementer of `assemble_e2_mutants.py` writes free-form strings for `mutation_op`; a downstream consumer (R1/R3/R4/R2 harnesses) that filters by `mutation_op` gets no portability guarantee.
The AC1 prove command checks key presence but not value membership — corpus rows with arbitrary strings pass the AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `mutation_op_vocab` object to `runs/e2_thresholds.json` mapping each class id to its canonical `mutation_op` string constant. Extend the AC1 prove snippet to assert `r['mutation_op'] in KNOWN_OPS` for each mutant row.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `run_e2_benchmark.py`'s design (and enforce in the harness) that a `rebar edit` injection failure aborts the affected pair with a non-zero exit (or logs a named error and excludes the pair from counting), and that the harness verifies the injected description matches the intended mutation before running the gate on each pair.
evidence: Plan states the harness 'injects each seeded defect into the clone via `rebar edit <id> --description=…`' with no error-handling guidance.
The crash-resumability guarantee ('journals raw verdicts incrementally, crash-resumable') only covers the verdict-emission phase after injection, not injection failure itself.
A partial injection leaves a ticket in the tmp store with its original (unmodified) description — a control masquerading as a mutant — which would silently corrupt the baseline table.
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a `rebar edit` injection failure during mutant-store population: the harness clones the real store and injects each seeded defect via `rebar edit <id> --description=…`, but if a single `rebar edit` call fails mid-run (e.g. lock contention, malformed description, CLI error), the plan does not specify whether to abort the run, skip the pair, or retry — leaving the tmp mutant store in an indeterminate partial-injection state.
findings.location: ## What — 'Gate-execution mechanism' / ## Scope — `run_e2_benchmark.py` description
scenarios: A `rebar edit` fails on pair 7 of 10 for class 2; the harness proceeds with the unedited ticket, scores it as a control-level result, and attributes it to the mutant slot — deflating recall without any error signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CI-collectable pytest unit test (e.g., `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_assembly.py`) exercising each class's mutation function on a synthetic base plan fixture — asserting the mutant differs from the control by exactly the seeded defect and no other field, and that the class-5 strip reliably removes the `[operator-attested]` tag while leaving everything else intact.
evidence: The plan specifies CI tests only for `e2_counting.py` and the `emit_baseline` seam in `test_e2_counting.py` and `test_e2_benchmark.py`
No pytest test for `assemble_e2_mutants.py`'s per-class mutation functions is mentioned anywhere in the plan
The class-5 construct-validity check is a one-liner proving command (`python3 -c …`) run at commit time, not a CI-collectable regression test
The `--dry-run --verify-clean` proving command exercises the filter logic but is not a pytest fixture
findings.finding: The mechanical mutation logic in `assemble_e2_mutants.py` — the per-class mutation functions that produce construct-valid mutants (e.g., layer-cake re-slicing for class 2, ordering-edge deletion for class 3, operator-attested tag stripping for class 5) — has no CI-collectable unit test. The only verification is post-hoc artifact inspection via proving commands (`--dry-run --verify-clean` and the class-5 one-liner). If a mutation function is subtly wrong (e.g., the class-5 strip misses a regex edge case, or the class-3 ordering-edge deletion leaves a residual edge), the committed corpus may be silently invalid, invalidating all downstream detection numbers.
findings.location: ## Scope — `harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py`; ## Acceptance Criteria — first AC item (corpus proving command)
scenarios: A regex edge case in the class-5 tag-strip function misses a multi-line `[operator-attested]` AC, so class-5 controls appear valid but mutants are identical to controls — the detection experiment is invalid but no CI test catches it
The class-3 ordering-edge deletion leaves a residual `depends_on` in a nested field, so the mutant is not actually defective — corpus is corrupt, but passes the artifact-inspection one-liner
findings.suggested_fix: Create `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_assembly.py` with synthetic base-plan dicts (no LLM, no live corpus) and test each of the 5 `apply_mutation_class_N` functions: assert the diff is exactly the expected field change, assert class-5 control contains `[operator-attested]` and its mutant does not, assert class-2/3 mutants have the right structural shape. Keep fixtures small (3-5 AC lines) — the goal is a regression safety net for the mutation logic, not full integration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the explicit top-level JSON schema for `e2_baseline.json` (and `e2_thresholds.json`) to the Scope or README section, stating that both files use `{"classes": {"1": {...}, ...}}` as their top-level structure.
evidence: AC 4 proving command: `b=json.load(open('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e2_baseline.json'))['classes']`
Scope: 'runs/e2_baseline.json — per-class detection/FP/indeterminate table' — no JSON schema shown
The e2_thresholds.json AC 3 proving command uses `t['classes']` consistently, so the schema is intended — but it is only implied, not stated.
findings.finding: The proving command in AC 4 accesses `e2_baseline.json` with `json.load(open(...))['classes']`, implying the file's top-level JSON object has a key named `'classes'`. However, the plan describes `e2_baseline.json` only as 'a per-class table with n_mutants, n_controls, detected, detection_rate, blocking_fp, fp_rate, indeterminate_mutants, indeterminate_controls, meets_threshold' — it never specifies the top-level JSON schema or the `'classes'` wrapper key. An implementor could produce `{"1": {...}, "2": {...}}` (flat) instead of `{"classes": {"1": {...}}}`, causing the proving command to raise `KeyError: 'classes'`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4; Scope — runs/e2_baseline.json description
scenarios: Executor writes e2_baseline.json as `{"1": {"n_mutants": 10, ...}, ...}` (flat, no 'classes' wrapper); AC 4's proving command raises KeyError and the AC cannot be proved even though the data is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `runs/e2_baseline.json`, add: 'Top-level structure: `{"classes": {"1": {n_mutants, n_controls, detected, detection_rate, blocking_fp, fp_rate, indeterminate_mutants, indeterminate_controls, meets_threshold}, ...}, "run_timestamp": "..."}`.  Similarly for `e2_thresholds.json`: `{"classes": {"1": {detection_min, blocking_fp_max}, ...}, "indeterminate_cap": 0.20}`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` flag description to the `assemble_e2_mutants.py` Scope entry, specifying that it cross-checks each selected base ticket's outcome_corpus.jsonl row against the 5-condition filter and exits non-zero naming the first violating ticket.
evidence: Scope says: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing' — no mention of `--verify-clean`

AC2 prove-command: `python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK` with parenthetical '(exits 0 only if every selected base ticket's corpus row passes the 5-condition filter; non-zero names the first violating ticket)'
ABSENCE: `--verify-clean` semantics are nowhere in the Scope or What body — only in AC2's parenthetical
findings.finding: The `--verify-clean` flag used in AC2's prove-command (`assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean`) is not described anywhere in the Scope or What sections. An implementer reading the Scope would know to implement `--dry-run` (described as 'prints the selected base pool without writing') but would have no specification for `--verify-clean` — its semantics (cross-check each base against corpus row, exit non-zero naming first violator) appear only in the AC parenthetical. This is a deliverable requirement present only in the AC, not in the plan body.
findings.location: Scope: `assemble_e2_mutants.py` description; AC2 prove-command
scenarios: Implementer builds `--dry-run` per Scope but omits `--verify-clean` entirely; AC2's prove-command fails; no guidance on what the flag should verify beyond the AC parenthetical
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for `assemble_e2_mutants.py`, add: '`--verify-clean` (usable with `--dry-run`): cross-checks every selected `source_ticket_id` against its `outcome_corpus.jsonl` row for all 5 filter conditions; exits non-zero and names the first violating ticket if any row fails.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pointer to the verified gate-run mechanism (the `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`+`REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`+`review_plan(..., sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)` contract) in either `AGENTS.md` or a `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/README.md`, so R1/R2/R3/R4 authors can find and reuse it without reading the full E2 writeup; also extend the README AC grep to assert a 'gate-run mechanism' heading is present.
evidence: Plan states R1/R2/R3/R4 each 're-run this harness with the candidate detector' but gives no cross-reference hook in AGENTS.md or the harness directory's own README.
README AC greps only for 'E2 — seeded-defect benchmark', 'run-validity', and 'degrad' — the 'verified gate-run mechanism' heading is required by the scope list but not verified by the AC grep.
Plan notes 'E2 introduces a new, verified gate-run mechanism' as distinct from all prior harnesses — this is a novel architectural contract.
findings.finding: The novel `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` + `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` store-injection gate-run mechanism is a reusable cross-component contract that R1, R2, R3, and R4 each consume when they 're-run this harness with the candidate detector.' It is documented only within the E2 README section, with no pointer added to AGENTS.md or a harness-level README. Future R-story authors must independently discover it inside E2's writeup, risking re-invention or misuse (e.g., forgetting `emit_sidecar=False` and polluting the sidecar corpus).
findings.location: ## Scope — `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md` AC; ## What — 'Gate-execution mechanism' subsection
scenarios: An R1 author writes their own gate-invocation loop, omits `emit_sidecar=False`, and silently writes REVIEW_RESULT sidecars into the corpus.
An R3 author duplicates the `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` clone logic with a subtly different store path, bypassing the isolation guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/README.md` (or a section in the existing README) with a 'Reusable gate-run mechanism' heading that captures the five required flags (`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`, `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`, `source="local"`, `sign=False`, `emit_sidecar=False`, `force=True`) and explains why each matters. Update the README AC grep to `grep -q 'gate-run mechanism' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/README.md`. Add a one-liner pointer in AGENTS.md under experiment harnesses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a retry/backoff strategy (e.g., exponential backoff with cap, skip-and-mark-indeterminate on repeated failure) for `review_plan()` API errors in `run_e2_benchmark.py`, and document the crash-resume cursor logic (how the harness detects already-completed rows in `e2_verdicts.jsonl` on restart).
evidence: Plan says 'journals raw verdicts incrementally (e2_verdicts.jsonl, crash-resumable)' but specifies no retry/backoff on `review_plan()` itself
'review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", repo_root=REAL_REPO, sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)' — no exception-handling wrapper described
Anthropic API is a hard external dep called ≥100 times sequentially
findings.finding: The harness calls `review_plan()` ≥100 times (≥10 mutants + 10 controls × 5 classes) against the Anthropic API, but the plan specifies no retry/backoff/skip strategy for API errors (rate-limit 429s, transient 500s, timeouts). An unhandled exception mid-batch would halt the harness; if the incremental journal was in a partially-written state at that moment, the crash-resume path may re-run or skip rows non-deterministically depending on how the journal cursor is managed — but the plan never specifies how the harness detects a previously-started-but-not-completed row.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism / ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: A transient 429 rate-limit error on call 47/100 raises an unhandled exception, halting the harness; on restart, the journal has 46 rows but the harness logic for detecting 'already done' rows is unspecified, risking duplicate processing or skipped rows.
A network timeout on a single call silently corrupts the verdicts journal if the write was partial.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `try/except` wrapper around each `review_plan()` call that retries up to 3× with exponential backoff on transient errors (429, 500, timeout), then marks the verdict as `INDETERMINATE` with a `error_reason` field if all retries fail; the crash-resume cursor should skip any `pair_id` already present in `e2_verdicts.jsonl` at startup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the tmp mutant store lifecycle: state whether it uses a fixed path or per-run tmpdir, add an explicit cleanup step (e.g., `shutil.rmtree` in a `finally` block) after each run, and add an AC or `--self-check-isolation` check that no stale mutations persist between runs.
evidence: 'the harness copies REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT/.tickets-tracker → TMP_MUTANT_STORE (a full clone)' — no cleanup step described
Plan is silent on whether TMP_MUTANT_STORE is a fixed path (reuse risk) or a per-run tmpdir (accumulation risk)
No AC verifies cleanup of the tmp store
findings.finding: The tmp mutant store (the `.tickets-tracker` clone written under `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`) has no stated RETIRE/cleanup path. The plan specifies CREATE (clone at start) and UPDATE (via `rebar edit` per mutant) but never states whether the tmp directory is deleted after a successful or failed run. Repeated benchmark runs would accumulate tmp clones if the directory is unique per run, or silently reuse a stale clone from a prior run if a fixed path is used.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism; ## Scope — run_e2_benchmark.py
scenarios: Harness uses a fixed `TMP_MUTANT_STORE` path; a second run reuses a stale clone already mutated from the prior run, making mutations additive rather than fresh, corrupting the benchmark.
Harness uses `tempfile.mkdtemp()` per run but never cleans up; repeated runs accumulate full `.tickets-tracker` clones (each potentially hundreds of MB), exhausting disk space on a CI runner.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix='e2_mutant_store_')` for the tmp store and wrap the harness main loop in `try/finally: shutil.rmtree(tmp_store, ignore_errors=True)` to guarantee cleanup on success and failure alike.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the raw `os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = ...` and `os.environ["REBAR_SYNC_PUSH"] = "off"` assignments in `run_e2_benchmark.py` with a context-manager that saves/restores both env vars (mirroring the `_sync_push_off()` pattern at `src/rebar/opcert_service/jobs.py:196-207`), to avoid env leaks on exception and to reuse the existing discipline rather than reinventing it.
evidence: Plan: `os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = TMP_MUTANT_STORE  # wins verbatim over repo_root for ALL ticket reads`
src/rebar/opcert_service/jobs.py:196-207: `_sync_push_off()` context manager saves prior value and restores in finally block for REBAR_SYNC_PUSH
The plan also sets REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off in the same snippet without save/restore
findings.finding: The harness code snippet in ## What sets `os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = TMP_MUTANT_STORE` as a plain assignment with no save/restore or context-manager guard, while the production codebase already defines an idiomatic context-manager pattern for exactly this kind of env-key sandboxing: `_sync_push_off()` in `src/rebar/opcert_service/jobs.py:196-207` uses save/restore with a proper `finally` block for `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH`. Mutating a global env var without restoring it is dangerous in a long-running harness (env leaks into subprocesses spawned after the finally block if an exception unwinds) and rebuilds a discipline the codebase already encodes.
findings.location: ## What — Gate-execution mechanism code snippet: `os.environ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR"] = TMP_MUTANT_STORE`
scenarios: If `review_plan()` raises mid-run, the harness process continues with a polluted REBAR_TRACKER_DIR pointing to a deleted tmp dir, causing silent failures in any subsequent code that reads the real store.
If the harness is run in a test process that also uses the real tracker, env leak crosses test isolation boundaries.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `_tracker_sandbox(tmp_store)` context manager in `run_e2_benchmark.py` (or import and adapt `_sync_push_off`) that saves/restores both `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` and `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH` in a `try/finally` block, then use `with _tracker_sandbox(TMP_MUTANT_STORE): verdict = review_plan(...)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `--verify-clean` and `--self-check-isolation` flag descriptions to the Scope section entries for `assemble_e2_mutants.py` and `run_e2_benchmark.py` respectively, specifying their exit conditions and what each checks.
evidence: AC2: `python3 docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean && echo FILTER-OK` — `--verify-clean` behavior not described in Scope
AC7: `.venv/bin/python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation` — `--self-check-isolation` behavior not described in Scope
Scope section for assemble_e2_mutants.py: '`--dry-run` prints the selected base pool without writing' — no mention of `--verify-clean`
Scope section for run_e2_benchmark.py: no mention of `--self-check-isolation`
findings.finding: The AC2 prove command calls `assemble_e2_mutants.py --dry-run --verify-clean` and the AC7 prove command calls `run_e2_benchmark.py --self-check-isolation`, but neither `--verify-clean` nor `--self-check-isolation` is defined anywhere in the Scope or What sections. The implementer cannot determine what these flags must do, what their exit conditions are, or what 'verify-clean' vs 'dry-run' checking entails beyond the terse prove-command context.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 prove command and AC7 prove command
scenarios: Implementer writes `--dry-run` logic but omits `--verify-clean` validation behavior since it's not scoped, so AC2 fails.
Implementer implements `--self-check-isolation` with wrong semantics (e.g., just prints 'OK' without running a gate) since the required behavior isn't specified.
findings.suggested_fix: In Scope under `assemble_e2_mutants.py`, add: '`--verify-clean` (paired with `--dry-run`): for each selected base ticket, assert its outcome_corpus.jsonl row satisfies all 5 filter conditions; exit non-zero naming the first violator.' Under `run_e2_benchmark.py`, add: '`--self-check-isolation`: run a single-pair dry gate run against the tmp clone and verify the real `.tickets-tracker` HEAD is unchanged; exit 0 if isolated, non-zero otherwise.'

findings.finding: The baseline table references `p6_ac_quality` as a criterion id to be 'validated against the live registry (`plan_review.registry.by_id`) at run time' — but `p6_ac_quality` is a DET-floor Python function name (in `det_floor.py:686`), NOT a `registry.by_id()` key; the actual DET-floor registry id is `P6`. A runtime validation against `registry.by_id()` would fail to find `p6_ac_quality` and either raise a KeyError or silently omit it.
findings.location: ## What — baseline table row for class 5; gate-run mechanism description
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `p6_ac_quality (det_floor.py:686)` with `P6` in the baseline table. Add a note that the runtime validation covers LLM-layer ids via `registry.by_id()` and DET-floor ids via `CANONICAL_DET` — these are separate namespaces.

findings.finding: AC6's prove command points to `test_e2_counting.py` for testing `run_e2_benchmark.emit_baseline`, but the Scope section defines `test_e2_counting.py` as covering only `e2_counting.classify_pair` and `e2_counting.run_validity`. `emit_baseline` is a function in `run_e2_benchmark.py`, not `e2_counting.py`. An implementer reading Scope will not add `emit_baseline` tests to `test_e2_counting.py`; an implementer reading AC6 will, but then the Scope's description of `test_e2_counting.py` is wrong.
findings.location: Scope (test_e2_counting.py entry) vs AC6 prove command
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_benchmark.py` entry to Scope covering the `emit_baseline` seam test, and update AC6's prove command to point to that file. Keep `test_e2_counting.py` scoped to the two pure helpers as Scope describes.

findings.finding: The AC for the `emit_baseline` enforcement seam test (`pytest tests/unit/experiments/test_e2_counting.py -k enforce`) locates the test in `test_e2_counting.py`, but `emit_baseline` is defined in `run_e2_benchmark.py` — a separate module from `e2_counting.py`. The scope section defines `e2_counting.py` as containing only the two pure helpers (`classify_pair` and `run_validity`), while `emit_baseline` is explicitly placed in `run_e2_benchmark.py`. The test file name suggests it tests `e2_counting.py` module contents only, creating ambiguity about whether `emit_baseline` tests must import across module boundaries into `test_e2_counting.py` or belong in a separate file.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC 6 (emit_baseline seam); ## Scope (e2_counting.py and run_e2_benchmark.py)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
